# **Design Document \- Persistence and Workspace State** 

Team 7 \- Sewe En Sestig

## Section (1-3) \- Sean

**1\. Overview**

iDaVIE (immersive Data Visualisation Interactive Explorer) is an open-source VR application developed by IDIA that lets astronomers load and explore 3D FITS data cubes in virtual reality, with tools for masking, feature cataloguing, and spectral analysis. In the original codebase, minimal persistence existed. The only thing that was saved was the mask FITS file. Nothing else was saved nor recovered in the event of a crash. This included states such as render settings, features, camera position and the data cube itself. Our team built a persistence layer for the project that captures the full state of a workspace session and serialises it to a JSON file. Crashes are detected and recovered by using a session lock file. Restoration/Loading has been implemented but has issues that need to be fixed. 

This document outlines how we satisfied each requirement we established in our requirements document, why we implemented certain design choices, our collaboration with the wider Team Alpha through our state contracts and our testing strategy.

**2\. Architecture**

The persistence layer follows a strict four-layer architecture. The Domain layer contains the workspace snapshot, all DTOs, and the serialisable POCO types. It has no Unity references and can be compiled and tested outside the engine entirely. The Application layer contains the orchestration logic: saving, restoring, autosave, crash detection, and validation. It also has no Unity references, depending only on interfaces. The Infrastructure layer handles the file system: JSON serialisation, the snapshot ring buffer, and schema migration. The Adapters layer is the only layer that references Unity. Each adapter reads from or writes to a live Unity MonoBehaviour and maps Unity types (Vector3, Quaternion, Color) to the plain C\# equivalents used by the DTOs.  
    
The reason domain and application are kept Unity-free is testability and long-term modifiability. Unity types cannot be instantiated in a plain NUnit test project, so any class that depends on them cannot be unit tested. Keeping the boundary at the adapter layer means the entire save/restore pipeline can be tested without the engine. 

**3\. Workspace Domain Model**

WorkspaceSnapshot \- the immutable point-in-time record of a session. Implemented as a sealed record rather than a plain class for two reasons: records support with expressions, which the serialiser uses in its two-pass checksum protocol to produce a modified copy with the checksum injected without mutating the original; and sealing it prevents inheritance from accidentally breaking the value semantics. It composes the five DTOs and a metadata envelope.

WorkspaceMetadata  the envelope written to the root of every snapshot file. Contains the schema version (semver string), UTC timestamp, SHA-256 checksum, the session profile, and two boolean flags: HasValidationWarnings (set when the validator auto-corrected something at save time) and PartialRecovery (set when not all sub-states could be restored). Storing these flags in the metadata means the application can warn the user on load without needing to re-validate the entire snapshot.

WorkspaceProfile — a four-value enum (DataOnly, DataWithMask, DataWithFeatures, FullWorkspace) inferred at save time by  
WorkspaceAggregate.InferProfile(). The design choice here was to avoid saving null sections for sub-systems that are not active. Without the profile, every snapshot would need to contain empty feature and foveation sections even for a simple data-only session. WorkspaceStubFactory uses the profile to allocate only the sub-sections that are relevant, which keeps snapshot files minimal and makes restore logic cleaner.

WorkspaceAggregate \- the mutable live-session counterpart to the immutable snapshot. The separation between aggregate and snapshot  
was a deliberate design decision: the aggregate is what the adapters write into during a running session, while the snapshot is  
what gets serialised to disk. Keeping them as separate types makes it impossible to accidentally serialise a partially-captured  
state, because Capture() must be called explicitly to produce the snapshot.

The five DTOs \- one per sub-system: DataIoStateDto, RenderingStateDto, InteractionStateDto, FeatureStateDto, GuiStateDto. Each property is annotated with \[PersistField\] or \[PersistField(Optional \= true)\], a custom attribute that makes the persistence contract explicit in the code itself rather than in a separate document. The Optional flag is used by WorkspaceValidator to decide whether a missing value should raise a warning or be silently skipped. Enums throughout the DTOs are stored as strings rather than integers so that snapshot files remain human-readable and so that reordering enum values in the source does not silently corrupt saved data.

Serialisable POCO types \- SerializableVector3, SerializableQuaternion, SerializableColor. These replace UnityEngine.Vector3, Quaternion, and Color at the domain boundary. The design choice to introduce these rather than using a JSON converter was deliberate: a converter would still require a Unity reference in the infrastructure layer, and would fail silently in a non-Unity test environment. By converting at the adapter boundary, the domain layer is completely free of Unity types, and the serialisation  
format is stable regardless of how Unity's internal representation changes between versions. SerializableQuaternion also includes a IsNotNormalised() check and a Normalised() method so the validator can detect and correct degenerate rotations before they reach the renderer.

## 

## Section (4-7)-- Jerry

**4\. State Capture**

## ![][image1]

The concrete implementation of each adaptor is the only part of the codebase that touches Unity types, the interfaces themselves serve as the bridge,   
capture() gets the state of the current unity session and sends it down the saving pipeline,   
restore() is the end point for loading a previous session back into unity  
The Original saving architecture was both **untestable** and **tightly coupled** Because ExitController directly instantiated and called into `VolumeDataSet` and `FeatureSetManager`, it could only run inside Unity and was coupled to the internals of multiple MonoBehaviours 

This new architecture fixes both issues, instead of the ExitController function reaching straight into volumeDataSet you have a DataIOAdapter which implements its interface. The data is still read from volumeDataSet, however the read is isolated and in one place. SaveWorkspaceUseCase calls The Workspace Collector interface and receives a work aggregate; it has no idea where that aggregate came from.

This means that you can test it by injecting stubs and it does not know the difference

**5\. State Store**

## 4 Step Saving Pipeline(SaveWorkSpaceUseCase.cs):

Saving Logic handled by the RunSave() function and executes as follows,

Sep 1: We collect all the data from the current session that we are going to store in our stub using the IWorkSpaceCollector which calls our 5 adaptors and produces a work aggregate.

Step 2: We fill our stubs using ‘with’, this creates an immutable copy of the stub which saves us from having to recall the constructor and fill it with mostly similar data, this way allows us to specify specific parts ,e.g DataIO, and change their values **only**. This is needed as our snapshot is stored as a record which does not allow post-instatiation definition of fields due to the face it needs to be safe to serialize and pass between threads.

Step 3: We then validate at save time. This allows us to flag any issues with the stub that is saved before it is written to disk. However it does NOT stop it from being written to the disk it is just flagged as having warnings

Step 4: it is serialised and pushed to the 10-slot FIFO ring buffer on disk using a SHA-256 two-pass checksum and  a .tmp file which is atomically renamed. The oldest slot is overwritten when full. This facilitates crash recovery as we cycle back through snapshots to find the newest valid state

\*the problem of both a manual save by the user and an autosave happening at the same time needed to be handled. We therefore implemented an execute() function that has a ‘save in progress’ variable. This is defined as ‘Volatile’ so that it gets written straight to main memory rather than being cached by the cpu. The try/finally guarantees it is always reset even if and exception is thrown.

**6\. State Restore**

## Loading Pipeline([LoadWorkSpaceUseCase.cs](http://LoadWorkSpaceUseCase.cs)):

Loading Logic is handled by TryLoad(): 

It first deserialises the Json(exception used here as Json should almost always be valid before entering)  
Checks if the schema is compatible and returns a fail if not   
Validates this snapshot(the same validation saving does)  
Returns a LoadResult object

The process of cycling through the ring buffer is handled by Execute():

Starts at the mostRecent snapshot and goes backwards from there  
It first checks if its empty  
Cycles through the buffer and calls the TryLoad() and stores the result, if the result is a fail it moves to the next most recent snapshot and tries that.

RestoreOrchestrator():   
Calls all the adaptors on the newly loaded Snapshot in the specific order of DataIO \-\> Rendering \-\> Features \-\> InteractionState \-\> Gui.  
This order reflects the dependency chain between sub-systems: DataIo must be restored first as all other sub-systems depend on the FITS dataset being present. Rendering depends on the loaded volume texture, Features depend on the FITS data for mask recomputation, Interaction needs a dataset to operate against, and GUI state is derived last as it depends on the domain state established by all previous steps.  
A clear Seperation of concerns is used here as we can see that LoadWorkspaceUseCase is only responsible for producing a validated snapshot(it does not touch Unity) while the RestoreOrchestrator is responsible for applying that snapshot back to the Unity adapters. 

**8\. Autosave**

Autosave is handled by AutosaveService, a Unity-free class in the Application layer. It runs a single System.Threading.Timer that fires every 20 seconds (the interval is passed in the constructor, so it can be changed later). On each tick it calls SaveWorkspaceUseCase.Execute() the same method a manual save uses. One save path means one thing to test and reason about.

The timer fires on a background thread, so an autosave and a manual save could overlap. Rather than re-solve this, the service relies on the volatile ‘save in progress’ guard already inside the use-case (Section 5): if a save is running when the timer fires, that tick is skipped and Execute() returns false. The skip rule lives in one place.

A manual save calls TriggerNow() instead of Execute() directly. This saves at once and then resets the timer, so the next autosave is a full interval away and we avoid a redundant save firing seconds after the user just saved. A SaveCompleted event reports whether each cycle saved or skipped, which the Unity side uses to show a ‘saved’ indicator without the Application layer knowing about the GUI.

FR6 asked for a 60-second cadence and an explicit dirty flag. The current build uses a fixed 20-second interval and always attempts a save (the use-case decides whether anything changed). The interval is configurable; the dirty flag is not yet implemented.

Member                  Purpose

| intervalSeconds (ctor) | Time between automatic saves. Injected, so it is configurable and testable. Defaults to 20\. |
| :---- | :---- |
| TriggerNow() | Manual-save entry point. Saves now and resets the timer to a full interval. |
| SaveCompleted (event) | Reports each cycle as saved or skipped, for UI feedback. |
| Start() / Stop() / Dispose() | Control and tear down the timer cleanly. |

### **9\. Crash Recovery**

## A clean shutdown can run cleanup code but a crash cannot. So instead of detecting a crash as it happens, we leave a marker on disk during normal use and treat its survival into the next launch as proof of a crash. This is the job of CrashDetector and a session.lock file.

## On startup the host calls CheckAndAcquire(). It records whether a lock file already exists, then writes a fresh one for this session (with the process ID and a UTC timestamp), and returns that earlier check. True means the last session never reached its clean-exit path, a crash so recovery is offered. False means a clean start. On clean exit the host calls Release() (also via Dispose()), which deletes the lock file. Only presence matters; the PID and timestamp are just diagnostics, so there is no parsing step that could fail.

## When a crash is detected, CrashRecoveryOrchestrator.Recover() takes over. It does not reimplement loading it calls LoadWorkspaceUseCase.Execute(), which walks the ring buffer newest-to-oldest and returns the first valid slot (Section 6). On success it wraps the result and builds a short message listing any applied warnings and excluded features. On failure it reports that a blank session is starting.

## Note on scope: FR7 named this service SessionRecoveryService and described a restore dialog. The built version splits the work into CrashDetector (the lock-file protocol) and CrashRecoveryOrchestrator (the recovery decision); the user-facing dialog is not yet wired in.

## 

##  

Startup                           Action

| No lock file | Clean previous exit. Start fresh and write a new lock file. |
| :---- | :---- |
| Lock file \+ valid snapshot | Crash. Recover from the newest valid slot and report any warnings. |
| Lock file \+ no valid snapshot | Crash, but all slots unusable. Start blank and tell the user. |
| Clean exit during the session | Release() deletes the lock file so the next launch is treated as clean. |

**10\. Schema Migration**

A snapshot saved by an older build must still load. Migration is built on one small interface, ISchemaMigration, with a FromVersion, a ToVersion, and a Migrate() method. Each migration handles exactly one version step, which keeps every one small and easy to test.

MigrationCoordinator holds these steps in order and exposes MigrateIfNeeded(). It reads the schema version from the snapshot, parses it as semver, and applies the compatibility rule: if the snapshot’s major version is higher than the current one it is rejected (written by a newer tool); an unparseable version is also rejected. Otherwise it walks the chain, applying each matching step until the snapshot reaches the current version (1.1.0). Minor and patch bumps are additive migrations just supply defaults for new fields.

The one shipped migration, Migration\_1\_0\_to\_1\_1, adds the TransformedSpectralValue field that 1.0.0 didn't have. It sets the field to 0.0 and bumps the version to 1.1.0; the DataIo adapter re-checks that value against the restored AST frame on load, so the default is a safe placeholder.

Current limitation: the chain is implemented and unit-tested (MigrationCoordinatorTests), but not yet called by the load pipeline LoadWorkspaceUseCase does not run MigrateIfNeeded() before validation. Wiring it in between deserialisation and validation is a known, single-point change. (FR8 named the types IMigrator and MigrationPipeline; the built equivalents are ISchemaMigration and MigrationCoordinator.)

**11\. Non-Functional Requirements**  
The layer was shaped by its non-functional requirements as much as its features. An invalid snapshot must never crash iDaVIE, so every load passes checksum and validation before any adapter runs. Writes are atomic (.tmp file then rename), so a snapshot is never left half-written. The 10-slot ring buffer scanned newest-to-oldest means one bad slot degrades to the previous good one. Keeping the domain and application layers Unity-free makes the whole pipeline testable in plain NUnit. The 20-second cadence and skip-in-progress guard bound how often the disk is touched.

NFR                           ISO/IEC 25010           Evidence

| NFR1 no Unity dependency | Maintainability (testability) | Domain and Application layers have no UnityEngine references; the save/load/validate/migrate pipeline runs in plain NUnit. |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| NFR2 boundaries are interfaces | Maintainability (modifiability) | IWorkspaceStateCollector, the five adapter interfaces, and ISchemaMigration are all interfaces backed by test doubles. |
| Robustness bad state never crashes | Reliability (fault tolerance) | SHA-256 check and WorkspaceValidator run before any adapter; invalid features excluded, missing fields defaulted. |
| Durability no half-written files | Reliability (recoverability) | Atomic .tmp-then-rename in SnapshotRing. |
| Recoverability survive a bad slot | Reliability (recoverability) | 10-slot ring scanned newest-to-oldest; blank-session fallback if none valid. |
| Performance bounded disk use | Performance efficiency | 20 s autosave interval; volatile guard skips overlapping saves. |

Refactored Example 1 — Workspace Snapshot vs SaveMask  
Old code to cite: VolumeDataSet.cs lines 1577–1595 (SaveMask) and 1481–1568 (SaveSubMask)

What the old save does:

Saves one thing only: mask voxel binary data to a FITS file  
Overwrites the same file every time — no history  
No integrity check — a crash mid-write produces a silently corrupt file  
Captures nothing about rendering, spatial transforms, features, or spectral system  
New code to cite:

File	Lines	What it shows  
refactoring-examples/Persistence/Domain/WorkspaceSnapshot.cs	1–21	The composite record — all 6 sub-states captured per save  
Assets/Scripts/Persistence/Adapters/RenderingStateAdapter.cs	51–125	Capture() showing the breadth: thresholds, colour map, transform, foveation, mask, moment maps  
refactoring-examples/Persistence/Infrastructure/SnapshotSerializer.cs	31–74	Two-pass SHA-256 checksum \+ atomic write  
refactoring-examples/Persistence/Infrastructure/SnapshotRing.cs	49–71	Ring buffer push — .tmp → atomic rename, 10-slot FIFO  
Argument for inclusion: SaveMask only preserves the mask file. A user who spends two hours adjusting colour maps, spatial transforms, and painting features loses all of that on exit — only the mask survives. The snapshot captures the complete session state in one write cycle so everything is recoverable.

Refactored Example 2 — Crash Detection vs nothing  
Old code: There is none. A crash loses everything with no recovery path — this is your argument.

New code to cite:

File	Lines	What it shows  
refactoring-examples/Persistence/Application/CrashDetector.cs	1–49 (entire file)	Session lock file protocol — CheckAndAcquire() detects crash on startup, Release() deletes lock on clean exit  
Argument for inclusion: iDaVIE sessions can last hours. SteamVR, GPU drivers, and system power events cause unclean exits that are outside the user's control. The lock file costs two file operations per session but gives a guaranteed recovery signal. The pattern is established practice — VSCode, databases, and most professional editors use the same mechanism. Without it, the ring buffer is useless because there is no way to know whether recovery is needed.
